extra.reasoning:
The property "globally (opted_out implies not sent)" is VIOLATED.

State machine: opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks (0-6). Initial: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0.

The vulnerability is transition [t5]: on send_attempt, if (opted_out and ticks >= 6), it sets sent := true. This directly produces a state where opted_out=true AND sent=true, contradicting the property. The only question is whether such a state is reachable and whether t5 can fire.

Reachability of ticks>=6: [t4] tick increments ticks while ticks<6 (capped at 6). Six tick events drive ticks from 0 to 6. Each tick also sets sent=false; opted_out stays false. Then [t1] optout sets opted_out=true, sent=false, leaving ticks=6 unchanged. Now both [t3] (send_attempt if opted_out -> sent=false) and [t5] (send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=6 -> sent=true) are enabled on a send_attempt. Since t5 is an enabled transition whose update sets sent=true while opted_out is true, there exists an execution that reaches a violating state. A safety property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, so the existence of this t5-firing execution refutes it.

Counterexample trace (8 events, within the 10-event bound):
- tick x6: ticks goes 0->1->2->3->4->5->6; sent=false; opted_out=false.
- optout (t1): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=6.
- send_attempt: t5 guard (opted_out=true and ticks>=6) holds -> sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=6.

This reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating "opted_out implies not sent." Semantically this is an opt-out leak: after enough time elapses (ticks>=6), a send_attempt re-enables sending to a patient who has opted out — precisely the hazard the property is meant to prevent.
